definition: Covenant-lite structures dominate >90% of leveraged loans, delaying default recognition by an estimated 12-24 months and lowering eventual recovery rates from historical ~70 cents to 40-50 cents. This creates a systematic late-cycle timing error in any model using default rates as a cycle-identification input.
evidence: S&P/Moody's data confirms defaults remain artificially low then spike
Recovery rates declined from historical ~70 cents to 40-50 cents
Less gradual rolling pattern than covenanted cycles
Meta-point: any default-rate-based model is structurally lagged
>90% covenant-lite is an observable market structure fact
15-40% EBITDA addback phenomenon overstates credit quality metrics
Delay-then-burst mechanism has structural analogues (LTCM, SVB — opacity creates information gaps resolving suddenly)
2020 episode too brief and rapidly reversed by policy to be fully informative
open_questions: Is the 12-24 month delay estimate robust given limited sample of cov-lite cycles?
How does cov-lite interact with private credit refinancing to further delay recognition?
What alternative signals can substitute for default rates in cycle identification?
What is the counterfactual Phillips Curve behavior without covenant-lite?
How much of the delay effect survives in market-implied distress measures (CDS-implied PD, option-adjusted spreads)?
When does the maturity wall force recognition?
